content:
It's been a few months since this happened. So, I can safely talk about it without any consequences. LinkedIn News India account put up the following year-end engagement post on, well, LinkedIn: -

Tech giants investing billions. Women's cricket blazing a trail. And making our mark even in space - it's been quite the year for India. So, what will 2026 bring?

Every December, LinkedIn News spotlights bold predictions poised to define the year ahead from our editors and experts around the world.

This year's lineup features a range of ideas - from Melinda French Gates' perspective on women's healthcare to AI's impact on performance reviews and entertainment. And not to forget, the emergence of a multipolar currency system and a one-person unicorn.

What trend do you think will have the biggest impact? Join the conversation in the comments or share your own prediction in a post or video with #Bigldeas2026.

Now, following were few of the responses (Names redacted for obvious reasons; No text is altered): -

By 2026, AI will become much more than just a tool. It will be a true partner for employees and HR teams alike.
Imagine context-aware, autonomous AI agents that can handle everything from onboarding to learning recommendations,
freeing up HR professionals and employees to focus on creative, strategic and people centric work.
This shift will help workplaces become more agile and personalized, with AI acting as a digital teammate that supports both individual growth and team collaboration.
As AI becomes more intuitive and human-like, HR will play a key role in ensuring ethical use, addressing questions of bias, privacy and fairness,
so that technology empowers people, rather than replacing them. In short, AI will help us reimagine not just how we work, but how we support and develop each other at work.

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

LinkedIn News India By 2026, the biggest AI shift won't be about smarter tools, but AI becoming a true work partner.
Instead of waiting for instructions, AI will understand context, anticipate needs,
and quietly handle complexity in the background - from planning and analysis to decision support.
The real breakthrough will be human impact: people spending less time managing work and more time thinking, creating, and leading.
The companies that win won't just adopt AI fastest, but use it most thoughtfully and responsibly.

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

The significant shift of 2026 won't just be models getting "smarter" or "faster".
It will be about AI moving from a reactive tool you use, to a proactive partner that works alongside you.
Here is what that shift looks like and why it changes everything.
1. Persistent Memory and "Hyper-Context"
2. True Cross-Application Agency
3. Goal-Oriented Behavior
4. Cross-App Agency
5. Anticipatory AI

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

The Rise of the "Non-Human Teammate" While 2024-2025 was about AI as a "copilot" (a tool we use), 2026 will be the year AI becomes a "teammate" (an agent we delegate to).
We will move from prompting AI for answers to assigning AI autonomous workflows. The biggest breakthrough won't be a faster model,
but the normalization of Agentic Workflows-where AI agents negotiate with other agents to execute complex tasks like supply chain adjustments, code deployment, or scheduling without human intervention.
Why this matters: It fundamentally changes the organizational chart. Managers in 2026 won't just manage people; they will manage "hybrid fleets" of human talent and autonomous AI agents.
The critical skill of 2026 will be "Agent Orchestration"- knowing how to lead a team that is 50% silicon.

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Every single one said the same thing. Nobody was copying each other. That's not what happened. What happened is simpler than that.

They copied the LinkedIn post into an LLM of their choice, got back three to five bullet points, cleaned up the formatting and hit post.

Their "prediction" was whatever their model generated. In response to a prompt they didn't write. About a future they didn't think about.

The Dead Internet Theory’s Cousin

The Dead Internet Theory argues that a large chunk of internet activity is already bots. Fake engagement, synthetic content, automated interaction designed to simulate a living web.

The Dead Creativity Theory, is what I dub the following phenomenon - The humans are still there. Still typing. Still posting. And you still can't tell. Not because they're bots. Because they've opted out of the part that would make them distinguishable from one.

The Excuse: Can't Nurture Creativity

Thinking is hard. Not everyone has something original to say about every topic, all the time.

Fair. But, that's not what happened. Nobody sat down, thought carefully about the next year in technology, found themselves at their wit's end. Then concluded that they had nothing original to add.

They didn't try and fall short. They didn't try. At all

The LinkedIn post was there. The LLM was one tab over. The path from post to comment took 45 seconds and 0 original thought. That wasn't a limitation. It was the laziest alternate choice.

The result was a comment section that looks like participation but contains nothing of value.

Dozens of people performing the act of having opinions without the inconvenience of forming any.

The Question: What’s Dying

Not the internet. Not intelligence. Not even creativity in some obscure sense. Just the Habit of it.

Original thought is slower. Sitting with an incomplete idea and the uncomfortable stretch between "I think something about this" and "I know what I think about this". It takes longer than a prompt-response cycle.

Think even less and the patience for it shrinks. Do it rarely enough and the instinct to try stops showing up at all.

That's my theory. Not that creativity is impossible. That, creativity is becoming the thing that people used to do before LLMs existed.

The Answer: Here's How to Revive

The real threat of LLMs isn't that they will surpass human imagination, it's that they make opting out of it so seamless.

When we outsource the messy, uncomfortable process of forming an opinion, we forfeit the very friction that makes human thought distinct.

All that just to save time and energy, while wasting litres of water and megawatts of electricity on AI tokens churning through a GPU sat in some data centre in some corner of this spherical world.

If we keep treating original thought as a chore to be automated away, we won't end up in a world ruled by superintelligent AI. We’ll just end up in a world where everyone sounds like the same prompt response on a Friday evening.
